summary: The four callback pointers written by the OTL writer tag handlers now resolve to concrete request-object behavior. Tags 0x4E2B and 0x4E6F install byte-stream append callbacks, tag 0x4EFB installs the integer progress/timeout callback, and tag 0x4E58 installs a floating-point progress thunk that normalizes double-valued progress arguments before tail-jumping into the integer callback.
updated_conclusion: The writer callback slots are libcurl-like transport callbacks rather than opaque async hooks. The primary body callback appends payload bytes into request +0x80, the secondary callback appends bytes into request +0x98, and the progress path maintains current/total counters plus a timeout-abort flag at request +0x210/+0x218/+0x220/+0x230/+0x234. This makes the OTL request object layout directly useful for OpenNXT 947 server bootstrap and response emulation work.

callback_slot_map.request_object_effect: Appends callback byte stream into request vector at request +0x80.
callback_slot_map.return_contract: Returns handled byte count computed as RDX * R8.

callback_slot_map.request_object_effect: Appends callback byte stream into request vector at request +0x98.
callback_slot_map.return_contract: Returns handled byte count computed as RDX * R8.

callback_slot_map.request_object_effect: Updates progress counters and timeout state at request +0x210/+0x218/+0x220/+0x230/+0x234.
callback_slot_map.return_contract: Returns non-zero when request +0x234 has been set, matching an abort/stop signal.

callback_slot_map.request_object_effect: Converts double-valued progress arguments to integer registers and tail-jumps to 0x14062BAF0.
callback_slot_map.return_contract: Inherited from 0x14062BAF0 after the tail jump.

callbacks.calling_convention_observation: RCX is the source buffer, RDX and R8 form the byte count, and R9 is the request/userdata pointer.
field_effects: Computes destination vector pointer as R9 + 0x80.
Computes existing vector size as [R9+0x88] - [R9+0x80].
Computes new byte count as RDX * R8.
Calls 0x140331210 to grow or reserve the vector to old_size + byte_count.
Calls 0x1407B9C90 to copy byte_count bytes from the source buffer to [R9+0x80] + old_size.
Returns byte_count in RAX.

callbacks.calling_convention_observation: Same callback shape as 0x14062BA10, but targets the second request vector.
field_effects: Computes destination vector pointer as R9 + 0x98.
Computes existing vector size as [R9+0xA0] - [R9+0x98].
Computes new byte count as RDX * R8.
Calls 0x140331210 to grow or reserve the vector to old_size + byte_count.
Calls 0x1407B9C90 to copy byte_count bytes from the source buffer to [R9+0x98] + old_size.
Returns byte_count in RAX.

callbacks.calling_convention_observation: RCX is the request pointer; R8 is the current progress value compared against request +0x210; RDX is persisted as a second progress/total value at request +0x218.
field_effects: Reads previous progress from request +0x210.
If the new current value in R8 is greater than the previous value, calls 0x1407CE184 and stores the returned millisecond timestamp at request +0x230.
If progress did not advance and request +0x220 is non-zero, computes request +0x220 seconds * 1000 + request +0x230 and compares that deadline to the current millisecond timestamp.
Sets request +0x234 to 1 when the timeout threshold has been exceeded.
Stores R8 to request +0x210 and RDX to request +0x218.
Returns AL = request +0x234 != 0.

callbacks.calling_convention_observation: This code lives in a .text gap without a pdata function record. It receives double progress values in XMM registers/stack slots, converts them to integer register arguments, restores RCX from R10, and tail-jumps to 0x14062BAF0.
field_effects: Converts the stack/XMM4 value to an integer and writes it back to [RSP+0x28].
Converts XMM3 into R9, XMM2 into R8, and XMM1 into RDX.
Uses 0x140BAC280 and 0x8000000000000000 to handle large double-to-integer conversion boundaries.
Tail-jumps to 0x14062BAF0 after argument normalization.
callbacks.decoder_tooling_note: This thunk required adding reusable SSE 0F 59/0F 5C ModRM decoding for MULSS/MULSD/MULPS/MULPD and SUBSS/SUBSD/SUBPS/SUBPD to the Reverser instruction decoder.

helper_roles.basis: Both byte append callbacks pass the vector object in RCX and requested size in RDX before copying new bytes.
Both append callbacks pass destination in RCX, source in RDX, and byte count in R8 immediately after growing the destination vector.
The progress callback stores its EAX return value at request +0x230 and compares later values against timeout_seconds * 1000 + +0x230.

neighbor_note.status: Not one of the four callback pointers.
neighbor_note.observed_role: Adjacent submitter-like helper that sets request state to 3, copies a string into request +0x8, prepares request +0x250, obtains a scheduler/queue object, enqueues through 0x14062CBE0, and stores a handle at request +0x240.

request_object_offsets.0x80: response body byte vector appended by callback 0x14062BA10
request_object_offsets.0x98: secondary/header byte vector appended by callback 0x14062BA80
request_object_offsets.0x220: timeout seconds or low-speed timeout threshold
request_object_offsets.0x234: timeout/abort flag returned by progress callback

opennxt_947_server_implications: Server-side bootstrap emulation should expect the client to accumulate response body bytes at request +0x80 and header-or-secondary bytes at request +0x98 before later parser stages consume them.
The callback return values matter: body/header callbacks return the exact byte count consumed, while the progress callback returns an abort flag derived from request +0x234.
Timeout behavior is now concrete enough to model: request +0x220 is multiplied by 1000 and compared against the elapsed time since the last progress timestamp at request +0x230.
The 0x4E58 thunk indicates the transport layer can use double-valued progress callbacks, but the internal request logic is integer-based after normalization into 0x14062BAF0.
The next server-facing win is tracing the consumers of request +0x80 and +0x98 so the raw accumulated response material can be connected to status, header, JS5, login, or bootstrap parsing.
next_targets: Trace parser consumers of request +0x80 and request +0x98 to classify body vs header/status handling.
Map 0x14062CBE0 and its queue/scheduler handoff so request +0x240 lifecycle and async ownership are named.
Follow request +0x234 abort propagation into the transport completion path.
Trace destructor/lifetime code for vectors at request +0x80 and +0x98 to confirm vector layout and ownership.
Promote the callback-pattern recognition into a native Reverser workflow once one more callback family uses the same byte-append/progress shape.
